meta.notes: File 03: OpenStax Microbiology Ch. 10 (Biochemistry of the Genome) and Ch. 11 (Mechanisms of Microbial Genetics). Ch.10 Q16 (eukaryotic genome 'structure') was skipped because multiple listed options are simultaneously true (ambiguous). Options 1-4 map to A-D; correctAnswer letters verified against the chapter answer keys.

questions.stem: Frederick Griffith infected mice with a combination of dead R and live S bacterial strains. What was the outcome, and why did it occur?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The mice will live. Transformation was not required.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The mice will die. Transformation of genetic material from R to S was required.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The mice will live. Transformation of genetic material from S to R was required.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The mice will die. Transformation was not required.

questions.explanation: Here the smooth (S) virulent strain is already alive, so it kills the mice on its own; the dead R cells are irrelevant and no transformation is needed. (Transformation only mattered in the classic case of dead S plus live R.)
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 10 Biochemistry of the Genome — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-10), Ch.10 MC Q1 = D.

hints.prompt: In bacterial genetics, 'transformation' refers to which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: One strain acquiring genetic material and taking on a new trait

choices.label: B
choices.text: A cell simply dividing in two

hints.rationale: Transformation is a cell acquiring genetic material and a new trait.

hints.prompt: Transformation is only RELEVANT when a living strain must GAIN a trait from dead cells. In a mixture that already contains living cells of the strain of interest, transformation is:

choices.label: A
choices.text: Not required for those living cells to show their trait

choices.label: D
choices.text: The only way any cell can function

hints.rationale: If living cells already possess the trait, no transformation is needed for them to express it.

hints.prompt: Which two options can be eliminated because they claim TRANSFORMATION was required, even though the deadly (virulent) strain is already alive and can act on its own?

choices.label: A
choices.text: the mice die because transformation of R to S was required, and the mice live because transformation of S to R was required

choices.label: B
choices.text: the mice live because transformation was not required, and the mice die because transformation was not required

choices.label: C
choices.text: the mice die because transformation of R to S was required, and the mice die because transformation was not required

choices.label: D
choices.text: the mice live because transformation of S to R was required, and the mice live because transformation was not required

hints.rationale: Both of these require transformation, which is unnecessary when a live virulent strain is already present.

questions.stem: Why was the alga Acetabularia a good model organism for Joachim Hämmerling to use to identify the location of genetic material?

choices.label: C
choices.text: It is a large, asymmetrical, single cell easy to see with the naked eye.

questions.explanation: Acetabularia is an unusually large single-celled alga with a distinctive shape, so Hämmerling could graft and transplant parts and observe the results with the naked eye.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 10 Biochemistry of the Genome — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-10), Ch.10 MC Q2 = C.

hints.prompt: Hammerling located the genetic material by cutting, grafting, and transplanting parts of the organism and watching what regenerated. This method requires an organism whose parts you can:
hints.rationale: Grafting and transplant experiments require parts you can manipulate and observe.

hints.prompt: Would producing a protein capsid help a researcher graft and observe a cell's parts?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No - a protein capsid is a virus feature, irrelevant to grafting a cell

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes - capsids make cells easier to cut

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes - capsids are required for grafting

hints.rationale: A protein capsid is a viral structure, unrelated to grafting and observing a cell.

hints.prompt: Which two features are irrelevant to being able to graft and observe a single cell (one is a reproductive trait, one is a virus feature)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: self-fertilization and making a protein capsid

choices.label: B
choices.text: being a large, easily seen single cell and lacking a nuclear membrane

choices.label: C
choices.text: self-fertilization and being a large, easily seen single cell

choices.label: D
choices.text: making a protein capsid and being a large, easily seen single cell

hints.rationale: Self-fertilization is a reproductive trait and a protein capsid is a viral structure; neither aids grafting or observation.

questions.stem: Which of the following best describes the results from Hershey and Chase's experiment using bacterial viruses with 35S-labeled proteins or 32P-labeled DNA that are consistent with protein being the molecule responsible for hereditary?

choices.label: A
choices.text: After infection with the 35S-labeled viruses and centrifugation, only the pellet would be radioactive.

choices.label: B
choices.text: After infection with the 35S-labeled viruses and centrifugation, both the pellet and the supernatant would be radioactive.

choices.label: C
choices.text: After infection with the 32P-labeled viruses and centrifugation, only the pellet would be radioactive.

choices.label: D
choices.text: After infection with the 32P-labeled viruses and centrifugation, both the pellet and the supernatant would be radioactive.

questions.explanation: If protein were the hereditary molecule, the 35S (protein) label would enter the bacterial cells; after centrifugation the radioactivity would be found only in the pellet (the cells). In reality the 32P (DNA) entered the cells, supporting DNA as the genetic material.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 10 Biochemistry of the Genome — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-10), Ch.10 MC Q3 = A.

hints.prompt: In the Hershey-Chase experiment, after centrifugation, what does the PELLET contain versus the SUPERNATANT?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The pellet holds the infected bacterial cells; the supernatant holds material left OUTSIDE the cells

choices.label: B
choices.text: The pellet holds only water; the supernatant holds the cells

choices.label: D
choices.text: The pellet holds light phage coats; the supernatant holds heavy cells

hints.rationale: Heavy infected cells sediment into the pellet; material left outside stays in the supernatant.

hints.prompt: In this experiment, which labeling is correct: what does 35S mark and what does 32P mark?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 35S marks protein (sulfur is in protein, not DNA); 32P marks DNA (phosphate is in DNA, not protein)

hints.rationale: Sulfur-35 labels protein and phosphorus-32 labels DNA.

hints.prompt: The question asks for a result supporting PROTEIN as the hereditary molecule. Which two options can be eliminated because they track the 32P (DNA) label, not the protein label?

choices.label: A
choices.text: after 32P infection only the pellet is radioactive, and after 32P infection both the pellet and supernatant are radioactive

choices.label: B
choices.text: after 35S infection only the pellet is radioactive, and after 35S infection both the pellet and supernatant are radioactive

choices.label: C
choices.text: after 35S infection only the pellet is radioactive, and after 32P infection only the pellet is radioactive

choices.label: D
choices.text: after 35S infection both are radioactive, and after 32P infection both are radioactive

hints.rationale: The 32P label tracks DNA, so it cannot demonstrate PROTEIN being the hereditary molecule.

questions.stem: Which method did Morgan and colleagues use to show that hereditary information was carried on chromosomes?

choices.label: A
choices.text: statistical predictions of the outcomes of crosses using true-breeding parents

choices.label: B
choices.text: correlations between microscopic observations of chromosomal movement and the characteristics of offspring

choices.label: C
choices.text: transformation of nonpathogenic bacteria to pathogenic bacteria

choices.label: D
choices.text: mutations resulting in distinct defects in metabolic enzymatic pathways

questions.explanation: Morgan's group correlated the inheritance of traits (e.g., eye color in Drosophila) with the microscopically observed behavior of specific chromosomes, supporting the chromosomal theory of inheritance.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 10 Biochemistry of the Genome — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-10), Ch.10 MC Q4 = B.

hints.prompt: To prove that genes are physically carried on chromosomes, the most convincing evidence would connect a gene's inheritance to what?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The physical behavior of a cell structure

hints.rationale: Linking inheritance to a physical cell structure's behavior ties genes to chromosomes.

hints.prompt: Bacterial transformation experiments (converting harmless bacteria to pathogenic) belong to which field?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Microbial/molecular genetics, not observation of chromosomes

hints.rationale: Bacterial transformation is a microbial/molecular approach, not chromosome observation.

hints.prompt: Which two methods come from MICROBIAL/biochemical genetics (using bacteria or enzyme pathways) rather than observing chromosomes in cells?

choices.label: A
choices.text: transformation of nonpathogenic bacteria to pathogenic, and mutations causing defects in metabolic enzyme pathways

choices.label: B
choices.text: correlating chromosome movement with offspring traits, and statistical predictions from true-breeding crosses

choices.label: C
choices.text: transformation of bacteria, and correlating chromosome movement with offspring traits

choices.label: D
choices.text: mutations in metabolic enzymes, and statistical predictions from true-breeding crosses

hints.rationale: Bacterial transformation and metabolic-enzyme mutations are microbial/biochemical approaches, not chromosome observation.

questions.stem: According to Beadle and Tatum's "one gene–one enzyme" hypothesis, which of the following enzymes will eliminate the transformation of hereditary material from pathogenic bacteria to nonpathogenic bacteria?
questions.explanation: DNA is the transforming material (Avery–MacLeod–McCarty). Deoxyribonucleases (DNases) degrade DNA, so treating with DNase would abolish transformation.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 10 Biochemistry of the Genome — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-10), Ch.10 MC Q5 = D.

hints.prompt: The molecule that carries hereditary information and acts as the 'transforming principle' in bacterial transformation is which of the following?
hints.rationale: DNA is the transforming principle carrying hereditary information.

hints.prompt: To ABOLISH transformation, you must destroy the transforming material. Which enzyme's job is to digest that material?

choices.label: A
choices.text: An enzyme that specifically digests DNA

hints.rationale: Since DNA is the transforming material, an enzyme that digests DNA abolishes transformation.

hints.prompt: Which two enzymes can be eliminated because they target non-nucleic-acid molecules (sugars and proteins), which are not the transforming material?
hints.rationale: Carbohydrate-degrading enzymes and proteinases attack sugars and proteins, not the DNA transforming material.

questions.stem: Which of the following is not found within DNA?
questions.explanation: DNA is built from nucleotides (sugar, phosphate, and bases such as thymine) joined by phosphodiester bonds with complementary base pairing. Amino acids are the building blocks of proteins, not DNA.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 10 Biochemistry of the Genome — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-10), Ch.10 MC Q6 = D.

hints.prompt: DNA is a polymer built from repeating units called what?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Nucleotides (a sugar, a phosphate, and a nitrogenous base)

hints.rationale: DNA is a polymer of nucleotides.

hints.prompt: Is thymine one of the four nitrogenous bases found in DNA?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes - thymine is one of the four DNA bases

choices.label: B
choices.text: No - thymine is found only in RNA

choices.label: D
choices.text: Thymine is a type of sugar

hints.rationale: Thymine is a DNA base, so it is genuinely found in DNA.

hints.prompt: Which two are genuine features found IN DNA, so both can be eliminated - one is a DNA base, the other links the backbone?

choices.label: B
choices.text: amino acids and complementary base pairing

hints.rationale: Thymine is a DNA base and phosphodiester bonds link the backbone; both are truly in DNA.

questions.stem: If 30% of the bases within a DNA molecule are adenine, what is the percentage of thymine?
questions.explanation: By Chargaff's rule, adenine pairs with thymine, so %A equals %T. If adenine is 30%, thymine is also 30%.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 10 Biochemistry of the Genome — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-10), Ch.10 MC Q7 = C.

hints.prompt: In DNA, adenine specifically base-pairs with which base?
hints.rationale: Adenine pairs with thymine (A-T).

hints.prompt: Chargaff's rules describe consistent relationships among the amounts of DNA bases. To predict the percentage of thymine, thymine's amount should be considered in relation to which base in particular?
hints.rationale: Thymine's percentage is determined in relation to its pairing partner, adenine.

hints.prompt: Which two percentages can be ruled out as arbitrary values not supported by any DNA base-composition rule?
hints.rationale: 20% and 35% follow from no base-composition rule; the remaining candidates are the base-pairing value and the common 'all bases equal 25%' misconception.

questions.stem: Which of the following statements about base pairing in DNA is incorrect?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Purines always base pairs with pyrimidines.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Base pairs are stabilized by hydrogen bonds.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Base pairing occurs at the interior of the double helix.

questions.explanation: The incorrect statement is that adenine binds guanine. Adenine pairs with thymine and guanine pairs with cytosine (purine–pyrimidine pairs held by hydrogen bonds inside the helix).
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 10 Biochemistry of the Genome — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-10), Ch.10 MC Q8 = B.

hints.prompt: In DNA, the two strands are held together by hydrogen bonds between paired bases located where?

choices.label: A
choices.text: In the interior of the double helix

choices.label: B
choices.text: On the outer surface of the helix

hints.rationale: Base pairs sit in the interior of the double helix.

hints.prompt: Which type of bond holds complementary DNA bases together?
hints.rationale: Complementary bases are held together by hydrogen bonds.

hints.prompt: The question wants the INCORRECT statement, so the true ones can be eliminated. Which two statements are TRUE about DNA base pairing?

choices.label: A
choices.text: base pairs are stabilized by hydrogen bonds, and base pairing occurs at the interior of the double helix

choices.label: B
choices.text: adenine binds to guanine, and purines always pair with pyrimidines

choices.label: C
choices.text: adenine binds to guanine, and base pairs are stabilized by hydrogen bonds

choices.label: D
choices.text: purines always pair with pyrimidines, and adenine binds to guanine

hints.rationale: Hydrogen-bond stabilization and interior pairing are both true, so neither is the incorrect statement.

questions.stem: If a DNA strand contains the sequence 5'-ATTCCGGATCGA-3', which of the following is the sequence of the complementary strand of DNA?
questions.explanation: The complementary strand is antiparallel and base-paired (A-T, G-C): the complement of 5'-ATTCCGGATCGA-3' read in the 5'→3' direction is 5'-TCGATCCGGAAT-3'.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 10 Biochemistry of the Genome — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax (prime symbols normalized to '). correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-10), Ch.10 MC Q9 = D.

hints.prompt: A complementary DNA strand is built by which rules?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Pair A with T and G with C, running antiparallel (opposite 5'-to-3' direction)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Pair A with G and C with T

choices.label: D
choices.text: Reverse the sequence but keep the same bases

hints.rationale: The complement pairs A-T and G-C and runs antiparallel.

hints.prompt: For the template 5'-ATTCCGGATCGA-3', which operation gives the correct complementary strand written 5' to 3'?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Replace each base with its partner (A with T, G with C), then read the result in the 5'-to-3' direction

choices.label: C
choices.text: Keep the same bases but just flip the end labels

choices.label: D
choices.text: Replace A with G and T with C

hints.rationale: Complement each base and read 5' to 3' to get the antiparallel partner.

hints.prompt: Which two options can be eliminated at a glance - one because it is IDENTICAL to the original strand (no pairing done), and one because it is written 3'-to-5' rather than the requested 5'-to-3'?
hints.rationale: One option merely repeats the original sequence and the other is written 3'-to-5', so both can be set aside.

questions.stem: During denaturation of DNA, which of the following happens?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hydrogen bonds between complementary bases break.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Phosphodiester bonds break within the sugar-phosphate backbone.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hydrogen bonds within the sugar-phosphate backbone break.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Phosphodiester bonds between complementary bases break.

questions.explanation: Denaturation separates the two strands by breaking the hydrogen bonds between complementary bases; the covalent phosphodiester backbone stays intact.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 10 Biochemistry of the Genome — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-10), Ch.10 MC Q10 = A.

hints.prompt: What does it mean to 'denature' a double-stranded DNA molecule?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Separate the two strands from each other without breaking the backbone

choices.label: B
choices.text: Cut the sugar-phosphate backbone into pieces

choices.label: C
choices.text: Change the bases into amino acids

hints.rationale: Denaturation separates the two strands, leaving each backbone intact.

hints.prompt: Denaturation (for example by heat) breaks the WEAKER interactions first while leaving strong COVALENT bonds intact. Which bonds in DNA are the weaker, more easily broken ones?

choices.label: D
choices.text: There are no weak bonds in DNA

hints.rationale: Hydrogen bonds are the weak interactions broken during denaturation; covalent backbone bonds survive.

hints.prompt: Which two options are wrong because they claim PHOSPHODIESTER (covalent backbone) bonds break during denaturation, when in fact the backbone stays intact?

choices.label: A
choices.text: phosphodiester bonds break within the sugar-phosphate backbone, and phosphodiester bonds between complementary bases break

choices.label: B
choices.text: hydrogen bonds between complementary bases break, and hydrogen bonds within the sugar-phosphate backbone break

choices.label: C
choices.text: hydrogen bonds between complementary bases break, and phosphodiester bonds break within the sugar-phosphate backbone

choices.label: D
choices.text: hydrogen bonds within the sugar-phosphate backbone break, and phosphodiester bonds between complementary bases break

hints.rationale: Denaturation leaves the covalent phosphodiester backbone intact, so any option claiming those bonds break is wrong.

questions.stem: Which of the following types of RNA codes for a protein?
questions.explanation: Messenger RNA (mRNA) carries the protein-coding sequence that ribosomes translate into a polypeptide.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 10 Biochemistry of the Genome — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-10), Ch.10 MC Q11 = B.

hints.prompt: For an RNA to 'code for a protein,' it must do which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Carry a sequence of codons that specifies the order of amino acids

choices.label: B
choices.text: Physically carry amino acids to the ribosome

choices.label: C
choices.text: Form the structural core of the ribosome

hints.rationale: A protein-coding RNA carries codons specifying the amino acid sequence.

hints.prompt: Which functional role matches an RNA that 'codes for a protein' (as opposed to building the ribosome or ferrying amino acids)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It serves as the readable message that the ribosome translates

choices.label: B
choices.text: It is the ribosome's structural and catalytic RNA

choices.label: C
choices.text: It is the adaptor that brings amino acids to match codons

choices.label: D
choices.text: It is a double-stranded RNA involved in regulation

hints.rationale: The coding RNA is the message the ribosome reads; the others are structural or adaptor RNAs.

hints.prompt: Which two RNA types have STRUCTURAL or ADAPTOR roles in translation (building the ribosome and carrying amino acids), rather than carrying the protein-coding message?
hints.rationale: rRNA builds the ribosome and tRNA carries amino acids; neither codes for the protein.

questions.stem: A nucleic acid is purified from a mixture. The molecules are relatively small, contain uracil, and most are covalently bound to an amino acid. Which of the following was purified?
questions.explanation: Small RNA molecules that contain uracil and are charged with (covalently bound to) an amino acid are transfer RNAs (tRNAs).
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 10 Biochemistry of the Genome — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-10), Ch.10 MC Q12 = D.

hints.prompt: A nucleic acid that contains URACIL (instead of thymine) must be which of the following?
hints.rationale: Uracil is found in RNA, not DNA.

hints.prompt: Which RNA is characteristically SMALL and becomes covalently 'charged' with a specific amino acid?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The small adaptor RNA that delivers amino acids during translation

choices.label: C
choices.text: The large structural RNA of the ribosome

hints.rationale: The small adaptor RNA (tRNA) is charged with an amino acid.

hints.prompt: Which two options can be eliminated - one because it is not RNA at all (it lacks uracil), and one because it is a LARGE structural RNA rather than a small amino-acid-carrying one?
hints.rationale: DNA contains thymine (not uracil) and rRNA is large and structural; neither fits a small, uracil-containing, amino-acid-charged molecule.

questions.stem: Which of the following types of RNA is known for its catalytic abilities?
questions.explanation: Ribosomal RNA (rRNA) acts as a ribozyme — the peptidyl transferase activity of the ribosome is catalyzed by rRNA.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 10 Biochemistry of the Genome — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-10), Ch.10 MC Q13 = C.

hints.prompt: An RNA molecule that can act as an enzyme (catalyze a reaction) is called what?
hints.rationale: A catalytic RNA is a ribozyme.

hints.prompt: The peptide-bond-forming (peptidyl transferase) activity of the ribosome is carried out by which component?
hints.rationale: Ribosomal RNA catalyzes peptide bond formation, acting as a ribozyme.

hints.prompt: Which two RNA types serve information or adaptor roles (carrying the message and delivering amino acids) rather than catalyzing a reaction?
hints.rationale: mRNA carries the coding message and tRNA delivers amino acids; neither is the catalytic RNA.

questions.stem: Ribosomes are composed of rRNA and what other component?
questions.explanation: Ribosomes are ribonucleoprotein complexes made of ribosomal RNA (rRNA) and ribosomal proteins.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 10 Biochemistry of the Genome — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-10), Ch.10 MC Q14 = A.

hints.prompt: Which of the following is a nucleic acid (like RNA), as opposed to another class of molecule?
hints.rationale: DNA is a nucleic acid; protein, carbohydrate, and lipid are other classes.

hints.prompt: Is DNA a structural component of the ribosome itself?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No - DNA stays in the genome and is not a structural part of the ribosome

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes - ribosomes are built partly from DNA

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes - DNA forms the ribosome's core

choices.label: D
choices.text: DNA and rRNA are the same molecule

hints.rationale: DNA is not a building block of the ribosome.

hints.prompt: Which two options are informational nucleic acids, not structural building blocks of the ribosome, so both can be eliminated?
hints.rationale: DNA and mRNA are informational nucleic acids, not structural parts of the ribosome.

questions.stem: Which of the following may use RNA as its genome?
questions.explanation: Only viruses can have RNA genomes; all cellular organisms (bacteria, archaea, eukaryotes) store their genetic information in DNA.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 10 Biochemistry of the Genome — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-10), Ch.10 MC Q15 = C.

hints.prompt: What kind of genome do all CELLULAR organisms (bacteria, archaea, eukaryotes) have?
hints.rationale: All cellular organisms store their genomes in DNA.

hints.prompt: Do bacteria store their genes in DNA or in RNA?
hints.rationale: Bacteria (like all cellular organisms) use DNA genomes.

hints.prompt: Which two options are PROKARYOTIC cellular organisms (which must use DNA genomes), so both can be eliminated?
hints.rationale: Bacteria and archaea are cellular (prokaryotic) organisms and store their genomes in DNA.

questions.stem: Which of the following is typically found as part of the prokaryotic genome?
questions.explanation: Plasmids — small, usually circular, extrachromosomal DNA molecules — are commonly part of prokaryotic genomes. Chloroplast and mitochondrial DNA are eukaryotic, and prokaryotic chromosomes are typically circular.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 10 Biochemistry of the Genome — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-10), Ch.10 MC Q17 = C.

hints.prompt: Chloroplasts and mitochondria are organelles found in which type of cell?
hints.rationale: Chloroplasts and mitochondria are eukaryotic organelles.

hints.prompt: Organelle DNAs (chloroplast and mitochondrial) sit inside their organelles. Can a cell that lacks those organelles contain that organelle DNA?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No - without the organelle, there is no organelle DNA

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes - organelle DNA floats freely in any cell

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes - all cells contain organelle DNA

choices.label: D
choices.text: Organelle DNA is identical to plasmid DNA

hints.rationale: No organelle means no organelle DNA, so prokaryotes lack chloroplast/mitochondrial DNA.

hints.prompt: Which two options are ORGANELLE DNAs found only in eukaryotic organelles that prokaryotes lack, so both can be eliminated?
hints.rationale: Chloroplast and mitochondrial DNA reside in eukaryotic organelles that prokaryotes do not have.

questions.stem: Serratia marcescens cells produce a red pigment at room temperature. The red color of the colonies is an example of which of the following?
questions.explanation: The observable red pigmentation is a phenotype — the expressed physical characteristic resulting from the organism's genotype and environment.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 10 Biochemistry of the Genome — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-10), Ch.10 MC Q18 = B.

hints.prompt: What is a 'phenotype'?

choices.label: A
choices.text: An observable physical characteristic of an organism

choices.label: B
choices.text: The complete DNA sequence of an organism

choices.label: C
choices.text: A permanent change to the DNA code

hints.rationale: A phenotype is an observable trait.

hints.prompt: The visible red color of the colonies is something you can directly SEE. Which category does an observable, visible trait fall into?

choices.label: B
choices.text: The underlying genetic code (sequence of alleles)

choices.label: C
choices.text: A mutation in the DNA sequence

choices.label: D
choices.text: A change in the organism's habitat

hints.rationale: A visible trait is an observable expressed characteristic.

hints.prompt: Which two options refer to the DNA/genetic code itself rather than an observable trait, so both can be eliminated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: genotype and change in DNA base composition

choices.label: B
choices.text: phenotype and adaptation to the environment

choices.label: D
choices.text: adaptation to the environment and change in DNA base composition

hints.rationale: Genotype and a change in DNA base composition both describe the genetic code, not the visible trait.

questions.stem: Which of the following genes would not likely be encoded on a plasmid?

choices.label: A
choices.text: genes encoding toxins that damage host tissue

choices.label: D
choices.text: genes encoding enzymes for the degradation of an unusual substrate

questions.explanation: Essential housekeeping genes such as those for glycolysis reside on the main chromosome. Plasmids typically carry accessory, situational genes (toxins, antibiotic resistance, specialized catabolism).
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 10 Biochemistry of the Genome — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-10), Ch.10 MC Q19 = C.

hints.prompt: How do genes on the main chromosome typically differ from genes on plasmids?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The chromosome carries essential housekeeping genes; plasmids carry optional/situational genes

choices.label: B
choices.text: The chromosome carries only useless genes; plasmids carry all vital ones

choices.label: D
choices.text: Plasmids carry the genes for basic metabolism and growth

hints.rationale: Essential housekeeping genes are chromosomal; plasmids hold accessory genes.

hints.prompt: Antibiotic-resistance genes are a textbook example of genes carried on which element?
hints.rationale: Antibiotic-resistance genes are classic plasmid-borne accessory genes.

hints.prompt: Which two are classic ACCESSORY genes often carried on plasmids (so they ARE plasmid-likely, not the exception)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: genes for antibacterial resistance and genes for toxins that damage host tissue

choices.label: B
choices.text: genes for glycolysis enzymes and genes for antibacterial resistance

choices.label: C
choices.text: genes for glycolysis enzymes and genes for toxins

choices.label: D
choices.text: genes for degrading an unusual substrate and genes for glycolysis enzymes

hints.rationale: Antibiotic resistance and toxin genes are classic plasmid-borne accessory genes.

questions.stem: Histones are DNA binding proteins that are important for DNA packaging in which of the following?
questions.explanation: Histones package DNA in eukaryotes and in many archaea. Bacteria generally use nucleoid-associated proteins rather than true histones.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 10 Biochemistry of the Genome — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-10), Ch.10 MC Q20 = D.

hints.prompt: Bacteria package their DNA using non-histone proteins. So a correct 'histone users' group should NOT include which domain?
hints.rationale: Bacteria lack true histones, so a histone-users group should exclude bacteria.

hints.prompt: Given that bacteria do not use true histones, any answer option that lists bacteria as a histone-packaging group must be:
hints.rationale: Any option that includes bacteria as histone users is wrong.

hints.prompt: Which two answer options can be eliminated because they INCLUDE bacteria, which do not use true histones?

choices.label: A
choices.text: archaea and bacteria, and bacteria and eukaryotes

choices.label: B
choices.text: eukaryotes and archaea, and double-stranded and single-stranded DNA viruses

choices.label: C
choices.text: archaea and bacteria, and eukaryotes and archaea

choices.label: D
choices.text: bacteria and eukaryotes, and double-stranded and single-stranded DNA viruses

hints.rationale: Both of these pairings include bacteria, which lack true histones, so both are wrong.

questions.stem: DNA does all but which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: serves as the genetic material passed from parent to offspring

choices.label: B
choices.text: remains constant despite changes in environmental conditions

choices.label: C
choices.text: provides the instructions for the synthesis of messenger RNA

choices.label: D
choices.text: is read by ribosomes during the process of translation

questions.explanation: Ribosomes read messenger RNA, not DNA, during translation. DNA is the heritable material, is relatively stable, and is transcribed into mRNA.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 11 Mechanisms of Microbial Genetics — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-11), Ch.11 MC Q1 = D.

hints.prompt: DNA is famously stable and heritable. Which statement reflects these well-known properties?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It is passed from parent to offspring and stays relatively constant

choices.label: C
choices.text: It is destroyed during every cell division

choices.label: D
choices.text: It is different in every cell of one organism

hints.rationale: DNA is heritable and relatively constant - both true properties.

hints.prompt: Why is DNA's relative stability important for an organism?

choices.label: A
choices.text: So genetic instructions are reliably preserved and passed on

choices.label: B
choices.text: So the cell can digest it for food

choices.label: C
choices.text: So it can change with every meal

hints.rationale: Stability lets DNA reliably preserve and transmit genetic instructions.

hints.prompt: The question asks what DNA does NOT do, so true statements can be eliminated. Which two statements are TRUE of DNA?

choices.label: A
choices.text: serves as the genetic material passed from parent to offspring, and remains constant despite changes in environmental conditions

choices.label: B
choices.text: is read by ribosomes during translation, and provides the instructions for synthesis of messenger RNA

choices.label: C
choices.text: serves as the genetic material passed from parent to offspring, and is read by ribosomes during translation

choices.label: D
choices.text: remains constant despite environmental conditions, and is read by ribosomes during translation

hints.rationale: DNA is heritable and relatively stable; both statements are true, so neither is the exception.

questions.stem: According to the central dogma, which of the following represents the flow of genetic information in cells?
questions.explanation: The central dogma describes information flowing from DNA to RNA (transcription) to protein (translation).
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 11 Mechanisms of Microbial Genetics — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-11), Ch.11 MC Q2 = B.

hints.prompt: In the cell, which molecule is the primary long-term store of genetic information that the other steps start from?
hints.rationale: DNA is the information store from which expression begins.

hints.prompt: In normal gene expression, can PROTEIN act as the starting template that is used to make nucleic acids?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No - protein is not normally used as a template to make DNA or RNA

hints.rationale: Protein is not a template for making nucleic acids, so a flow starting with protein is wrong.

hints.prompt: Which two options can be eliminated because they do NOT begin with DNA (the information store)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: protein to DNA to RNA, and RNA to DNA to protein

choices.label: B
choices.text: DNA to RNA to protein, and DNA to protein to RNA

choices.label: C
choices.text: protein to DNA to RNA, and DNA to protein to RNA

choices.label: D
choices.text: RNA to DNA to protein, and DNA to RNA to protein

hints.rationale: Both of these start with protein or RNA, not DNA, so neither fits the central dogma's starting point.

questions.stem: Which of the following is the enzyme that replaces the RNA nucleotides in a primer with DNA nucleotides?
questions.explanation: In bacteria, DNA polymerase I removes the RNA primer and fills the gap with DNA nucleotides; DNA polymerase III is the main replicative polymerase.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 11 Mechanisms of Microbial Genetics — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-11), Ch.11 MC Q3 = B.

hints.prompt: During DNA replication, the short starter 'primer' is made of RNA. What must happen to this RNA primer before replication is complete?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It must be removed and replaced with DNA nucleotides

choices.label: B
choices.text: It must be left permanently in the new strand

choices.label: C
choices.text: It must be translated into protein

choices.label: D
choices.text: It must be exported from the cell

hints.rationale: The RNA primer is removed and replaced by DNA.

hints.prompt: The enzyme that replaces the RNA primer must both remove RNA nucleotides and add DNA nucleotides. Which general class of enzyme adds DNA nucleotides?

choices.label: C
choices.text: A primase (which only makes RNA primers)

hints.rationale: Only a DNA polymerase can add DNA nucleotides; in bacteria, DNA polymerase I also removes the RNA primer.

hints.prompt: Which two enzymes can be eliminated because they do NOT add DNA nucleotides at all (one only unwinds the helix, one only synthesizes the RNA primer)?

choices.label: B
choices.text: DNA polymerase I and DNA polymerase III

hints.rationale: Helicase only unwinds DNA and primase only makes RNA primers; neither adds DNA nucleotides.

questions.stem: Which of the following is not involved in the initiation of replication?
questions.explanation: DNA ligase acts later, sealing nicks and joining Okazaki fragments. DNA gyrase, single-stranded binding proteins, and primase all act during initiation/unwinding.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 11 Mechanisms of Microbial Genetics — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-11), Ch.11 MC Q4 = A.

hints.prompt: During the INITIATION/unwinding phase of DNA replication, what needs to happen?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The helix is unwound, the separated strands are stabilized, and primers are laid down

choices.label: B
choices.text: Okazaki fragments are sealed into one continuous strand

choices.label: C
choices.text: The finished DNA is packaged for cell division

hints.rationale: Initiation involves unwinding, stabilizing single strands, and priming.

hints.prompt: During initiation, which enzyme MAKES the short RNA primer needed to start synthesis?
hints.rationale: Primase synthesizes the RNA primer at initiation.

hints.prompt: Which two proteins clearly ACT during initiation (one makes the starting primer, one coats and stabilizes the separated single strands), so neither is the exception?
hints.rationale: Primase makes the initiating primer and single-stranded binding protein stabilizes the opened strands - both act at initiation.

questions.stem: Which of the following enzymes involved in DNA replication is unique to eukaryotes?
questions.explanation: Telomerase extends the linear chromosome ends (telomeres) of eukaryotes. Helicase, DNA polymerase, and ligase are common to prokaryotes and eukaryotes.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 11 Mechanisms of Microbial Genetics — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-11), Ch.11 MC Q5 = D.

hints.prompt: Why do EUKARYOTES need a special replication enzyme, related to chromosome ENDS, that prokaryotes generally do not?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Eukaryotic chromosomes are LINEAR and have ends (telomeres) that shorten and need maintenance

choices.label: B
choices.text: Eukaryotic chromosomes are circular with no ends

hints.rationale: Linear eukaryotic chromosomes have ends (telomeres) that require a special maintenance enzyme.

hints.prompt: Which enzyme's specific job is to extend and maintain the telomere ENDS of linear chromosomes?
hints.rationale: The telomere-extending enzyme (telomerase) maintains chromosome ends.

hints.prompt: Which two enzymes are used in BOTH prokaryotic and eukaryotic replication (so neither is unique to eukaryotes)?
hints.rationale: Helicase and DNA polymerase are common to both prokaryotic and eukaryotic replication.

questions.stem: Which of the following would be synthesized using 5'-CAGTTCGGA-3' as a template?
questions.explanation: The new strand is antiparallel and complementary to the template 5'-CAGTTCGGA-3', giving 3'-GTCAAGCCT-5'.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 11 Mechanisms of Microbial Genetics — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax (prime symbols normalized; option A's '4'' is reproduced as printed in the source). correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-11), Ch.11 MC Q6 = C.

hints.prompt: A newly synthesized DNA strand relates to its template strand how?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It is complementary (A-T, G-C) and antiparallel to the template

choices.label: B
choices.text: It is an identical copy of the template

choices.label: C
choices.text: It pairs A with G and C with T

choices.label: D
choices.text: It is the template read backward with the same bases

hints.rationale: The new strand is complementary and antiparallel to the template.

hints.prompt: To build the new strand for template 5'-CAGTTCGGA-3', what should you do to each base and to the direction?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Replace each base with its complement and write the strand antiparallel (its 3' end opposite the template's 5' end)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Copy each base unchanged and keep the same direction

choices.label: C
choices.text: Replace A with G and T with C, keeping the same direction

choices.label: D
choices.text: Only reverse the direction without changing any base

hints.rationale: Complement each base and orient the strand antiparallel to the template.

hints.prompt: Which two options can be eliminated on sight - one because it lists an impossible strand-end label ('4') and one because it simply repeats the template's own base sequence (uncomplemented)?
hints.rationale: One option has an invalid '4' terminus and another just repeats the template sequence, so both can be set aside.

questions.stem: During which stage of bacterial transcription is the σ subunit of the RNA polymerase involved?
questions.explanation: The sigma (σ) factor directs RNA polymerase to the promoter to begin transcription; it functions during initiation and then dissociates during elongation.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 11 Mechanisms of Microbial Genetics — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-11), Ch.11 MC Q7 = A.

hints.prompt: The sigma factor of bacterial RNA polymerase functions to do which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Recognize the promoter and position RNA polymerase at the transcription start site

choices.label: B
choices.text: Add nucleotides throughout the body of the gene

hints.rationale: Sigma factor recognizes the promoter to start transcription.

hints.prompt: Recognizing the promoter and positioning the polymerase to START occurs during which phase of transcription (after which the sigma factor detaches)?

choices.label: B
choices.text: The middle, while the RNA is being extended

choices.label: C
choices.text: The end, when the RNA is released

choices.label: D
choices.text: A phase that does not exist in bacteria

hints.rationale: Promoter recognition occurs at the start (initiation) phase.

hints.prompt: Which two options can be eliminated - one because it is not a step of BACTERIAL transcription at all, and one because it is the FINAL (release) step, opposite to a start-site function?
hints.rationale: Splicing is not part of bacterial transcription and termination is the final release step, so neither fits a promoter-recognition role.

questions.stem: Which of the following components is involved in the initiation of transcription?
questions.explanation: Transcription begins when RNA polymerase binds the promoter. Primers and origins belong to replication, and the start codon is a feature of translation.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 11 Mechanisms of Microbial Genetics — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-11), Ch.11 MC Q8 = C.

hints.prompt: Which process produces an RNA copy of a gene?
hints.rationale: Transcription makes an RNA copy of a gene.

hints.prompt: A 'start codon' (AUG) is a feature used in which process?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Translation (reading mRNA to build a protein)

hints.rationale: A start codon is used in translation, not transcription.

hints.prompt: Which two components belong to processes OTHER than transcription - one to translation (a start codon) and one to DNA replication (an origin)?
hints.rationale: A start codon belongs to translation and an origin to replication; neither is a transcription-initiation component.

questions.stem: Which of the following is not a function of the 5' cap or 3' poly-A tail of a mature eukaryotic mRNA molecule?

choices.label: C
choices.text: to aid export of the mature transcript to the cytoplasm

choices.label: D
choices.text: to aid ribosome binding to the transcript

questions.explanation: The 5' cap and 3' poly-A tail protect the mRNA, aid nuclear export, and promote ribosome binding. Splicing is carried out by the spliceosome, not by the cap or tail.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 11 Mechanisms of Microbial Genetics — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-11), Ch.11 MC Q9 = A.

hints.prompt: The 5' cap and poly-A tail sit at the two ENDS of an mRNA. This end position makes them well-suited to do what?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Protect the mRNA's ends and help it be handled, exported, and translated

choices.label: B
choices.text: Cut and rearrange the interior sequence of the mRNA

hints.rationale: End structures protect and help handle/export/translate the mRNA.

hints.prompt: mRNA processing includes several separate events (capping, tailing, and splicing) carried out by different machinery. This means a job done by one machine is:

choices.label: A
choices.text: Not necessarily done by another machine

hints.rationale: Different processing steps use different machinery, so one machine's job is not necessarily another's.

hints.prompt: Which two ARE genuine functions of the 5' cap or poly-A tail (so, since the question wants the NON-function, they can be eliminated)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: preventing mRNA degradation, and aiding export of the transcript to the cytoplasm

choices.label: B
choices.text: facilitating splicing, and aiding ribosome binding to the transcript

choices.label: C
choices.text: facilitating splicing, and preventing mRNA degradation

choices.label: D
choices.text: aiding ribosome binding, and facilitating splicing

hints.rationale: Protecting the mRNA from degradation and aiding nuclear export are both real cap/tail functions.

questions.stem: Mature mRNA from a eukaryote would contain each of these features except which of the following?
questions.explanation: Introns are spliced out during mRNA processing, so mature eukaryotic mRNA contains exon-encoded RNA, a 5' cap, and a 3' poly-A tail, but not intron-encoded RNA.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 11 Mechanisms of Microbial Genetics — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-11), Ch.11 MC Q10 = B.

hints.prompt: A mature eukaryotic mRNA has a protective structure added to its 5' end. What is added there?
hints.rationale: A 5' cap is added to the 5' end of a mature mRNA.

hints.prompt: The coding parts of a gene that are kept and expressed in the mature mRNA are called what?
hints.rationale: Exons are the kept, expressed coding portions of the mature mRNA.

hints.prompt: Which two features are definitely PRESENT in a mature mRNA (so both can be eliminated as the exception) - one is the coding sequence kept from the gene, the other is the protective 5' structure?

choices.label: B
choices.text: intron-encoded RNA and the 3' poly-A tail

hints.rationale: Exon-encoded RNA and the 5' cap are both present in mature mRNA.

questions.stem: Which of the following is the name of the three-base sequence in the mRNA that binds to a tRNA molecule?
questions.explanation: A codon is the three-nucleotide unit of mRNA that base-pairs with the complementary anticodon of a tRNA during translation.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 11 Mechanisms of Microbial Genetics — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-11), Ch.11 MC Q11 = B.

hints.prompt: How many nucleotides make up one 'word' of the genetic code that specifies a single amino acid?
hints.rationale: The genetic code is read in three-nucleotide units.

hints.prompt: What does the 'P site' of a ribosome refer to?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A location on the ribosome that holds a tRNA

choices.label: B
choices.text: A three-base sequence on the mRNA

hints.rationale: The P site is a ribosome location, not a three-base sequence.

hints.prompt: Which two options name a physical SITE or structure rather than a three-base sequence, so both can be eliminated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: the P site and the CCA binding site

choices.label: D
choices.text: the CCA binding site and anticodon

hints.rationale: The P site is a ribosome location and the CCA binding site is a tRNA structural end; neither is a three-base mRNA sequence.

questions.stem: Which component is the last to join the initiation complex during the initiation of translation?
questions.explanation: Translation initiation assembles the small subunit, mRNA, and initiator tRNA first; the large ribosomal subunit joins last to complete the initiation complex.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 11 Mechanisms of Microbial Genetics — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-11), Ch.11 MC Q12 = C.

hints.prompt: Which components must come together to form a translation initiation complex?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The two ribosomal subunits, the mRNA, and the initiator tRNA

choices.label: B
choices.text: Only the mRNA and a DNA template

choices.label: D
choices.text: Only tRNA and free amino acids

hints.rationale: Initiation assembles both ribosomal subunits, the mRNA, and the initiator tRNA.

hints.prompt: The small ribosomal subunit is one of the FIRST pieces that binds the mRNA to begin initiation. Given it binds first, can it be the LAST to join?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No - it is among the first, not the last

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes - it is always the last to join

choices.label: D
choices.text: It is not part of initiation

hints.rationale: The small subunit binds early, so it is not the last to join.

hints.prompt: Which two components are part of the EARLY initiation complex (they bind before the last piece), so neither is the last to join?

choices.label: A
choices.text: the small ribosomal subunit and the mRNA molecule

choices.label: B
choices.text: the large ribosomal subunit and the initiator tRNA

choices.label: C
choices.text: the small ribosomal subunit and the large ribosomal subunit

choices.label: D
choices.text: the mRNA molecule and the large ribosomal subunit

hints.rationale: The small subunit and mRNA are part of the initial complex, joining before the last component.

questions.stem: During elongation in translation, to which ribosomal site does an incoming charged tRNA molecule bind?
questions.explanation: Incoming aminoacyl- (charged) tRNAs enter at the A (aminoacyl) site; the growing peptide is held at the P site, and deacylated tRNAs exit through the E site.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 11 Mechanisms of Microbial Genetics — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-11), Ch.11 MC Q13 = A.

hints.prompt: The ribosome has three tRNA-binding sites. What are they?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The A (aminoacyl), P (peptidyl), and E (exit) sites

choices.label: B
choices.text: The A, B, and C sites

choices.label: C
choices.text: The start, middle, and end sites

hints.rationale: The ribosome's tRNA sites are A, P, and E.

hints.prompt: What is the role of the E site?

choices.label: A
choices.text: To release the spent (empty) tRNA as it exits

choices.label: B
choices.text: To accept the incoming charged tRNA

choices.label: C
choices.text: To hold the growing peptide chain

hints.rationale: The E (exit) site releases the spent tRNA.

hints.prompt: Which two options can be eliminated - one because it is where SPENT tRNA exits, and one because it is not a real ribosomal site at all?

choices.label: A
choices.text: the E site and the B site

choices.label: B
choices.text: the A site and the P site

choices.label: C
choices.text: the A site and the E site

choices.label: D
choices.text: the P site and the B site

hints.rationale: The E site is the exit site and there is no real 'B site'; neither accepts the incoming charged tRNA.

questions.stem: Which of the following is the amino acid that appears at the N-terminus of all newly translated prokaryotic and eukaryotic polypeptides?
questions.explanation: Translation begins at the AUG start codon, which codes for methionine (formylmethionine in bacteria), so methionine is the initial N-terminal residue of newly made polypeptides.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 11 Mechanisms of Microbial Genetics — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-11), Ch.11 MC Q14 = B.

hints.prompt: Translation of every polypeptide begins at which mRNA codon?

choices.label: B
choices.text: A stop codon such as UAA

hints.rationale: Translation initiates at the AUG start codon.

hints.prompt: The start codon AUG specifies which amino acid, making it the first residue placed at the N-terminus?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The amino acid encoded by AUG

choices.label: B
choices.text: Whatever amino acid is most abundant in the cell

choices.label: C
choices.text: The amino acid encoded by the stop codon

choices.label: D
choices.text: A different random amino acid each time

hints.rationale: AUG encodes methionine, the initial N-terminal residue.

hints.prompt: Which two are ordinary amino acids that are NOT encoded by the start codon, so neither is the universal first residue?
hints.rationale: Tryptophan and glycine are standard amino acids but are not encoded by the AUG start codon.

questions.stem: When the ribosome reaches a nonsense codon, which of the following occurs?

choices.label: D
choices.text: the A site binds to a charged tRNA

questions.explanation: A nonsense (stop) codon is recognized by a release factor rather than a tRNA, triggering termination and release of the completed polypeptide.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 11 Mechanisms of Microbial Genetics — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-11), Ch.11 MC Q15 = B.

hints.prompt: A 'nonsense codon' is another name for which kind of codon?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A stop codon that signals the end of translation

choices.label: D
choices.text: A codon that codes for two amino acids at once

hints.rationale: A nonsense codon is a stop codon signaling termination.

hints.prompt: Methionine is the amino acid added at the START of translation. At a STOP codon (the end), would a methionine be incorporated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No - that happens at initiation, not at a stop codon

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes - stop codons add a methionine

hints.rationale: Methionine is added at the start, not at a stop codon.

hints.prompt: Which two options describe ADDING to the polypeptide (incorporating an amino acid or forming a new peptide bond), which cannot happen at a stop codon?

choices.label: A
choices.text: a methionine is incorporated, and a peptide bond forms

choices.label: B
choices.text: the polypeptide is released, and the A site binds a charged tRNA

choices.label: C
choices.text: the polypeptide is released, and a peptide bond forms

choices.label: D
choices.text: a methionine is incorporated, and the polypeptide is released

hints.rationale: Incorporating an amino acid and forming a peptide bond are chain-building steps that do not occur at a stop codon.

questions.stem: Which of the following is a change in the sequence that leads to formation of a stop codon?
questions.explanation: A nonsense mutation changes a sense codon into a premature stop codon, truncating the protein. A missense mutation swaps one amino acid, and a silent mutation does not change the amino acid.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 11 Mechanisms of Microbial Genetics — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-11), Ch.11 MC Q16 = B.

hints.prompt: A point mutation that changes an amino-acid-coding codon into a STOP codon (truncating the protein) is called what?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A mutation that introduces a premature stop signal

choices.label: B
choices.text: A mutation that swaps one amino acid for another

choices.label: C
choices.text: A mutation with no change to the protein

choices.label: D
choices.text: A mutation that shifts the reading frame

hints.rationale: A mutation creating a premature stop is a nonsense mutation.

hints.prompt: The stem specifically wants a change that produces a STOP codon. How does that differ from a missense mutation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A stop-producing change halts translation early, whereas a missense change only substitutes one amino acid

choices.label: C
choices.text: A stop-producing change never affects the protein

choices.label: D
choices.text: A missense change always creates a stop codon

hints.rationale: A stop-producing change truncates the protein; missense merely substitutes an amino acid.

hints.prompt: Which two mutation types do NOT create a premature stop codon (one merely substitutes an amino acid, one causes no change at all)?
hints.rationale: A missense mutation substitutes an amino acid and a silent mutation changes nothing; neither creates a stop codon.

questions.stem: The formation of pyrimidine dimers results from which of the following?
questions.explanation: Ultraviolet (UV) light causes adjacent pyrimidines (especially thymines) to covalently bond, forming pyrimidine (thymine) dimers that distort the DNA.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 11 Mechanisms of Microbial Genetics — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-11), Ch.11 MC Q17 = C.

hints.prompt: A pyrimidine dimer forms when two adjacent bases become covalently bonded. This kind of damage is caused by a form of:

choices.label: C
choices.text: A chemical inserted into the DNA

hints.rationale: Pyrimidine dimers are caused by a form of light/electromagnetic radiation.

hints.prompt: Intercalating agents are chemicals that slip between base pairs. Do they cause pyrimidine dimers?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No - they cause insertions/deletions, not dimers

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes - they are the main cause of dimers

hints.rationale: Intercalators cause insertions/deletions, not pyrimidine dimers.

hints.prompt: Which two causes produce OTHER kinds of DNA damage (a base mismatch or an inserted/deleted base), not pyrimidine dimers?

choices.label: A
choices.text: spontaneous errors by DNA polymerase and exposure to intercalating agents

choices.label: B
choices.text: exposure to ultraviolet radiation and exposure to gamma radiation

choices.label: C
choices.text: spontaneous errors by DNA polymerase and exposure to ultraviolet radiation

choices.label: D
choices.text: exposure to intercalating agents and exposure to gamma radiation

hints.rationale: Polymerase errors cause mismatches and intercalators cause insertions/deletions; neither forms pyrimidine dimers.

questions.stem: Which of the following is an example of a frameshift mutation?
questions.explanation: Inserting or deleting a number of nucleotides not divisible by three shifts the reading frame. Deleting one nucleotide is a frameshift; deleting a whole codon (three nucleotides) does not shift the frame.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 11 Mechanisms of Microbial Genetics — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-11), Ch.11 MC Q18 = D.

hints.prompt: A frameshift mutation happens when the number of nucleotides inserted or deleted is:
hints.rationale: A frameshift results from an indel that is not a multiple of three.

hints.prompt: A missense mutation substitutes one base for another. Does a substitution change the total NUMBER of nucleotides (and thus shift the reading frame)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No - a substitution keeps the same length, so it does not shift the frame

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes - substitutions always shift the frame

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes - substitutions delete a whole codon

hints.rationale: A substitution does not change length, so it does not cause a frameshift.

hints.prompt: Which two changes involve NO change in the number of nucleotides (only a substitution or no change), so neither shifts the reading frame?

choices.label: B
choices.text: deletion of a codon and deletion of one nucleotide

choices.label: C
choices.text: missense mutation and deletion of one nucleotide

choices.label: D
choices.text: silent mutation and deletion of a codon

hints.rationale: A missense and a silent mutation each keep the same nucleotide count, so neither is a frameshift.

questions.stem: Which of the following is the type of DNA repair in which thymine dimers are directly broken down by the enzyme photolyase?
questions.explanation: Photolyase uses light energy to directly reverse (break apart) thymine dimers — an example of direct repair, which does not remove or replace nucleotides.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 11 Mechanisms of Microbial Genetics — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-11), Ch.11 MC Q19 = A.

hints.prompt: In 'direct' DNA repair, the damage is fixed how?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The lesion is chemically reversed in place, with no nucleotides removed

choices.label: B
choices.text: A stretch of nucleotides is cut out and resynthesized

choices.label: D
choices.text: The DNA is translated into protein

hints.rationale: Direct repair reverses the lesion in place without excising nucleotides.

hints.prompt: Photolyase uses light energy to split apart the bond of a thymine dimer WITHOUT cutting out any nucleotides. This mechanism is an example of which category?

choices.label: A
choices.text: In-place reversal of the damage (no nucleotides removed)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Cutting out a patch of nucleotides and filling the gap

choices.label: C
choices.text: Correcting a mispaired base after replication

choices.label: D
choices.text: The polymerase catching its own error as it synthesizes

hints.rationale: Splitting the dimer in place without excision is direct (in-place) repair.

hints.prompt: Which two are separate repair PATHWAYS that recognize a damaged or mispaired region and excise a stretch of the strand to replace it?

choices.label: A
choices.text: nucleotide excision repair and mismatch repair

choices.label: C
choices.text: direct repair and nucleotide excision repair

hints.rationale: Nucleotide excision repair and mismatch repair each recognize a problem region and excise a stretch of DNA to resynthesize.

questions.stem: Which of the following regarding the Ames test is true?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It is used to identify newly formed auxotrophic mutants.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It is used to identify mutants with restored biosynthetic activity.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It is used to identify spontaneous mutants.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It is used to identify mutants lacking photoreactivation activity.

questions.explanation: The Ames test screens for mutagens by detecting reversion of histidine-auxotrophic Salmonella back to histidine prototrophy — i.e., mutants with restored biosynthetic activity.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 11 Mechanisms of Microbial Genetics — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-11), Ch.11 MC Q20 = B.

hints.prompt: The Ames test is used primarily to determine whether a chemical is a:

choices.label: A
choices.text: Mutagen (a substance that can cause mutations)

hints.rationale: The Ames test screens substances for being mutagens.

hints.prompt: Photoreactivation is a light-driven DNA repair process. Is the Ames test a measure of a strain's photoreactivation (UV-repair) ability?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No - the Ames test measures mutation, not UV-repair capacity

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes - it requires sunlight to work

hints.rationale: The Ames test measures mutagenicity, not photoreactivation.

hints.prompt: Which two options can be eliminated - one because it describes creating a nutrient REQUIREMENT (the opposite of what the Ames test scores), and one because it concerns UV-repair ability rather than mutation detection?

choices.label: A
choices.text: identifying newly formed auxotrophic mutants, and identifying mutants lacking photoreactivation activity

choices.label: B
choices.text: identifying mutants with restored biosynthetic activity, and identifying spontaneous mutants

choices.label: C
choices.text: identifying newly formed auxotrophic mutants, and identifying mutants with restored biosynthetic activity

choices.label: D
choices.text: identifying spontaneous mutants, and identifying mutants lacking photoreactivation activity

hints.rationale: Creating auxotrophs is the opposite of the assay, and photoreactivation is unrelated to detecting mutagenicity.

questions.stem: Which is the mechanism by which improper excision of a prophage from a bacterial chromosome results in packaging of bacterial genes near the integration site into a phage head?
questions.explanation: Specialized transduction occurs when a prophage excises imprecisely, carrying adjacent bacterial genes into new phage particles that then transfer those genes.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 11 Mechanisms of Microbial Genetics — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-11), Ch.11 MC Q21 = C.

hints.prompt: Which mode of horizontal gene transfer uses a bacteriophage (virus) to carry bacterial DNA between cells?
hints.rationale: Transduction uses a phage to move bacterial DNA.

hints.prompt: Conjugation transfers DNA through a direct pilus connection between two cells. Does it involve a bacteriophage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No - conjugation uses a pilus, not a phage

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes - conjugation is a type of virus

choices.label: D
choices.text: Conjugation uses only free environmental DNA

hints.rationale: Conjugation uses a pilus, not a phage.

hints.prompt: Which two mechanisms do NOT use a bacteriophage at all (one uses a pilus, one takes up free DNA), so both can be eliminated?
hints.rationale: Conjugation uses a pilus and transformation takes up naked DNA; neither uses a phage.

questions.stem: Which of the following refers to the uptake of naked DNA from the surrounding environment?
questions.explanation: Transformation is the uptake and incorporation of free (naked) DNA from the environment by a competent cell.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 11 Mechanisms of Microbial Genetics — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-11), Ch.11 MC Q22 = D.

hints.prompt: Which term describes a bacterium taking up FREE (naked) DNA directly from its surroundings?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Uptake of free DNA from the environment by a competent cell

choices.label: B
choices.text: DNA transfer through a direct cell-to-cell pilus connection

choices.label: C
choices.text: DNA carried between cells inside a bacteriophage

hints.rationale: Taking up naked environmental DNA is transformation.

hints.prompt: The key phrase is 'naked DNA from the environment' - no phage and no cell-to-cell contact. Which description matches?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A competent cell absorbs loose DNA fragments present in its surroundings

choices.label: B
choices.text: A virus injects the DNA into the cell

choices.label: C
choices.text: Two cells join via a pilus to pass DNA

choices.label: D
choices.text: A prophage excises and carries adjacent genes

hints.rationale: Absorbing loose environmental DNA (no phage, no contact) is transformation.

hints.prompt: Which two mechanisms both require a bacteriophage to carry the DNA (so neither is uptake of naked environmental DNA)?
hints.rationale: Both generalized and specialized transduction use a phage to carry DNA, not free environmental DNA.

questions.stem: The F plasmid is involved in which of the following processes?
questions.explanation: The fertility (F) plasmid encodes the sex pilus and machinery for conjugation, the direct cell-to-cell transfer of DNA.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 11 Mechanisms of Microbial Genetics — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-11), Ch.11 MC Q23 = A.

hints.prompt: The F (fertility) plasmid provides the genes to build a sex pilus. A pilus physically does what?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Absorbs free DNA from the environment

choices.label: D
choices.text: Copies DNA within a single cell

hints.rationale: A pilus connects two bacteria to each other.

hints.prompt: Transduction moves bacterial DNA using which vehicle?

choices.label: B
choices.text: A pilus built by the F plasmid

choices.label: D
choices.text: A jumping gene within one genome

hints.rationale: Transduction uses a phage, not the F plasmid's pilus.

hints.prompt: Which two processes do NOT use a cell-to-cell pilus connection (one uses a phage, one uses free environmental DNA)?
hints.rationale: Transduction uses a phage and transformation uses naked DNA; neither uses the F plasmid's pilus.

questions.stem: Which of the following refers to the mechanism of horizontal gene transfer naturally responsible for the spread of antibiotic resistance genes within a bacterial population?
questions.explanation: Conjugation, often mediated by resistance (R) plasmids, is the main route by which antibiotic-resistance genes spread between bacteria.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 11 Mechanisms of Microbial Genetics — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-11), Ch.11 MC Q24 = A.

hints.prompt: Antibiotic-resistance genes in bacteria are frequently carried on which mobile genetic element that can be passed between cells?
hints.rationale: Resistance genes are often carried on plasmids (R plasmids).

hints.prompt: Resistance (R) plasmids spread most efficiently between bacteria by which mechanism of direct transfer?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Direct cell-to-cell transfer of the plasmid through a pilus

choices.label: C
choices.text: Release and uptake of naked DNA

choices.label: D
choices.text: A gene jumping within a single genome

hints.rationale: R plasmids spread by direct pilus-mediated transfer (conjugation).

hints.prompt: Which two mechanisms rely on a bacteriophage to move the DNA (rather than direct plasmid transfer between cells)?
hints.rationale: Both generalized and specialized transduction use a phage, not direct plasmid transfer.

questions.stem: An operon of genes encoding enzymes in a biosynthetic pathway is likely to be which of the following?
questions.explanation: Biosynthetic (anabolic) operons are usually repressible: they are transcribed by default but switched off when the end product accumulates (e.g., the trp operon).
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 11 Mechanisms of Microbial Genetics — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-11), Ch.11 MC Q25 = B.

hints.prompt: What is the difference between an inducible and a repressible operon?

choices.label: A
choices.text: An inducible operon is normally OFF and switched on; a repressible operon is normally ON and switched off

choices.label: C
choices.text: An inducible operon can never be turned on

choices.label: D
choices.text: A repressible operon is off permanently

hints.rationale: Inducible operons are normally off (induced on); repressible operons are normally on (repressed off).

hints.prompt: A biosynthetic (anabolic) operon makes enzymes that produce an end product. What should happen to these genes once plenty of the end product has accumulated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They should be switched OFF (no need to keep making the product)

choices.label: B
choices.text: They should stay permanently ON regardless of product level

choices.label: C
choices.text: They should require the end product as an inducer to turn ON

choices.label: D
choices.text: They should be deleted from the genome

hints.rationale: When the end product is abundant, a biosynthetic operon should be turned off (it is repressible).

hints.prompt: Which two options can be eliminated - one because it is the pattern typical of CATABOLIC (substrate-degrading) operons, and one because it describes the NUMBER of genes rather than a regulation pattern?
hints.rationale: 'Inducible' fits catabolic operons and 'monocistronic' refers to gene number, so neither describes a biosynthetic operon's regulation.

questions.stem: An operon encoding genes that are transcribed and translated continuously to provide the cell with constant intermediate levels of the protein products is said to be which of the following?
questions.explanation: Constitutive genes are expressed continuously at a relatively constant level, regardless of regulatory signals.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 11 Mechanisms of Microbial Genetics — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-11), Ch.11 MC Q26 = C.

hints.prompt: Gene expression can be REGULATED (switched on/off by signals) or UNREGULATED (running at a steady baseline all the time). Continuous, steady expression regardless of signals is which kind?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Tightly switched on only by an inducer

choices.label: C
choices.text: Tightly switched off by a corepressor

hints.rationale: Continuous steady expression is unregulated (baseline) expression.

hints.prompt: The stem says the genes are transcribed and translated CONTINUOUSLY to give CONSTANT levels. Does this describe a signal-responsive operon or a steady always-on one?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A steady, always-on operon that does not wait for a signal

choices.label: B
choices.text: An operon that turns on only when an inducer is present

choices.label: C
