choices.text: An operon that turns off when an end product accumulates

choices.label: D
choices.text: An operon that must be activated by a special protein each time

hints.rationale: Constant, continuous expression describes a steady always-on operon.

hints.prompt: Which two options are the two classic OPPOSITE regulatory logics of operons - one normally OFF until induced, the other normally ON until repressed?
hints.rationale: Inducible (off until induced) and repressible (on until repressed) are the two classic opposite logics; a continuously expressed operon is neither.

questions.stem: Which of the following conditions leads to maximal expression of the lac operon?
questions.explanation: The lac operon is maximally expressed when lactose is present (inducer removes the repressor) and glucose is absent (low glucose raises cAMP, so CAP activates transcription).
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 11 Mechanisms of Microbial Genetics — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-11), Ch.11 MC Q27 = A.

hints.prompt: The lac operon is negatively controlled by a repressor. What must happen to that repressor for transcription to occur at all?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The repressor must be removed/inactivated, which requires the inducer (lactose)

choices.label: B
choices.text: The repressor must bind the operator more tightly

choices.label: D
choices.text: The repressor is irrelevant to lac control

hints.rationale: Transcription needs the repressor inactivated, which requires the inducer (lactose).

hints.prompt: The lac operon is also POSITIVELY controlled by CAP, an activator that boosts transcription strongly. What does an activator protein do when it is active?
hints.rationale: An activator (CAP) boosts transcription to maximal levels when it is active.

hints.prompt: Which two conditions can be eliminated because, with LACTOSE ABSENT, no inducer is available and the repressor keeps the operon off?

choices.label: A
choices.text: lactose absent with glucose absent, and lactose absent with glucose present

choices.label: B
choices.text: lactose present with glucose absent, and lactose present with glucose present

choices.label: C
choices.text: lactose absent with glucose absent, and lactose present with glucose present

choices.label: D
choices.text: lactose present with glucose absent, and lactose absent with glucose present

hints.rationale: Without lactose there is no inducer, so the repressor keeps the operon off; both lactose-absent conditions can be eliminated.

questions.stem: Which of the following is a type of regulation of gene expression unique to eukaryotes?
questions.explanation: Histone modification (an epigenetic/chromatin-level control) is characteristic of eukaryotes. Attenuation, alternate sigma factors, and alarmones are prokaryotic regulatory mechanisms.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 11 Mechanisms of Microbial Genetics — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-11), Ch.11 MC Q28 = C.

hints.prompt: A major difference: eukaryotes wrap their DNA around histone proteins into chromatin, while bacteria do not. Chromatin is therefore unique to which cells?
hints.rationale: Histone-based chromatin is a eukaryotic feature.

hints.prompt: Sigma factors are subunits of BACTERIAL RNA polymerase. Are they a eukaryote-unique feature?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No - sigma factors are bacterial, not eukaryote-unique

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes - only eukaryotes have sigma factors

choices.label: D
choices.text: Sigma factors are found only in viruses

hints.rationale: Sigma factors are a bacterial feature, so an alternate sigma factor is not eukaryote-unique.

hints.prompt: Which two mechanisms are BACTERIAL/prokaryotic (one is a subunit of bacterial RNA polymerase, one is a bacterial stress-signaling molecule), so both can be eliminated as not eukaryote-unique?

choices.label: A
choices.text: use of an alternate sigma factor and alarmones

choices.label: B
choices.text: chemical modification of histones and attenuation

choices.label: D
choices.text: chemical modification of histones and use of an alternate sigma factor

hints.rationale: Alternate sigma factors and alarmones are bacterial mechanisms, not eukaryote-unique.
